coredata.dc:title: A near-infrared phototheranostic probe for in vivo hyaluronidase detection and activatable imaging-guided synergistic therapy of triple negative breast cancer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.

HU, Rongrong
ZHANG, Xinyu
LI, Jiaojiao

ZHANG, Chengwu
YAN, Lili
GUO, Lixia
MA, Sufang
DIAO, Haipeng
coredata.dc:description: Activatable phototheranostic probes may become novel option for tumor diagnosis and treatment due to their abilities to accurately detect cancer and guide therapeutic intervention through imaging. In this study, a hyaluronidase (HAase)-responsive near-infrared phototheranostic probe (HA-ICG-PDA) has been developed by covalently coating indocyanine green (ICG)-decorated hyaluronic acid (HA) onto the surface of polydopamine (PDA) for the imaging and therapy of triple negative breast cancer (TNBC). The probe exhibits sensitive and selective fluorescence enhancement to HAase, enabling the detection of HAase and activatable near-infrared fluorescence imaging of HAase-overexpressed tumor cells. Moreover, the combination of ICG and PDA equips HA-ICG-PDA with outstanding photodynamic and enhanced photothermal effects upon 808 nm laser irradiation. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments have confirmed that HA-ICG-PDA is capable of realizing imaging diagnosis of TNBC through HAase-activated fluorescence signal. With the guidance of fluorescence imaging, the probe displays synergistic photothermal and photodynamic therapy effects against TNBC, with minimal damage to the normal tissues. Therefore, the proposed phototheranostic probe paves a new avenue for HAase detection, and subsequently, diagnosis and imaging-guided treatment of TNBC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phototheranostic probe
Hyaluronidase
Near-infrared fluorescence imaging
Synergistic treatment
Triple negative breast cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis optimization and characterization of HA-ICG-PDA
Spectroscopic responses of HA-ICG-PDA to HAase
In vivo fluorescence and thermal imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by NSF of China (Nos. 82071969, and 21705104); the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (No. 2019L0406); the Youth Top Talent Plans of Shanxi Province (No. 20180606); Open Fund from Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University), Ministry of Education, China (No. KLMEC/SXMU-202003); and the Startup Foundation for Doctors of Shanxi Medical University (No: 03201601).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (insert) and DLS analysis of HA-ICG-PDA. Scale bar, 100 µm. (B) The absorption spectra of HA, ICG, PDA, HA-ICG, and HA-ICG-PDA. (C) The fluorescence spectra of HA-ICG-PDA with and without HAase (500 µg/mL).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of HA-ICG-PDA reacting with different concentrations of HAase (0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 70, 100, 400, and 500 µg/mL). (B) Linear fitting curve of ΔF against the concentration (C) of HAase between 10 and 45 µg/mL. λ ex/em = 750/805 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of DCFH-DA in the reaction system of HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) and HAase (500 µg/mL) under 808 nm laser irradiation for varied periods (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 min). λ ex = 485 nm. (B) Temperature curves and (C) thermal images of HA-ICG -PDA at different concentrations under 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2, 10 min). (D) Temperature curves and (E) thermal images of HA-ICG-PDA (60 µg/mL) under 808 nm laser with varied power densities for 10 min. (F) Temperature changes of HA-ICG-PDA during five cycles of repeated on-off laser irradiation.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of different cell lines upon incubation with HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) for 6 h. Scale bar, 20 µm. (B) Relative pixel intensity (red channel) of the corresponding images in panel A.

The viability of (A) MDA-MB-231, (B) MCF-7, and (C) MCF 10A cells upon treatment by HA-ICG-PDA with or without laser irradiation. (D) Live/dead staining analysis of MDA-MB-231 cells after different treatments. Scale bar, 100 µm.

(A) In vivo fluorescence images of tumor-bearing mice at different intervals after intravenous injection of HA-ICG-PDA. (B) In vivo thermal images of tumor-bearing mice with different treatments. a and b, intravenous injection of PBS (a) or HA-ICG-PDA (b) without laser irradiation; c and d, intravenous injection of PBS (c) or HA-ICG-PDA (d) 8 h prior to 808 nm laser irradiation.

(A) Schematic diagram of the therapy course. (B) Photographs and (C) tumor growth curves of mice in different groups during therapy. (D) Photographs and (E) weights of the residual tumor tissues dissected from the mice after treatment. (F) Body weight changes of mice in different groups during therapy. Error bars come from n = 3 measurements. *p < 0.001.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of HA-ICG-PDA, fluorescence response to HAase, and FI-guided PDT/PTT towards CD44 overexpressed TNBC.

head.ce:title: A near-infrared phototheranostic probe for in vivo hyaluronidase detection and activatable imaging-guided synergistic therapy of triple negative breast cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key laboratory of Cellular Physiology, Ministry of Education, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University), Ministry of Education, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Activatable phototheranostic probes may become novel option for tumor diagnosis and treatment due to their abilities to accurately detect cancer and guide therapeutic intervention through imaging. In this study, a hyaluronidase (HAase)-responsive near-infrared phototheranostic probe (HA-ICG-PDA) has been developed by covalently coating indocyanine green (ICG)-decorated hyaluronic acid (HA) onto the surface of polydopamine (PDA) for the imaging and therapy of triple negative breast cancer (TNBC). The probe exhibits sensitive and selective fluorescence enhancement to HAase, enabling the detection of HAase and activatable near-infrared fluorescence imaging of HAase-overexpressed tumor cells. Moreover, the combination of ICG and PDA equips HA-ICG-PDA with outstanding photodynamic and enhanced photothermal effects upon 808 nm laser irradiation. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments have confirmed that HA-ICG-PDA is capable of realizing imaging diagnosis of TNBC through HAase-activated fluorescence signal. With the guidance of fluorescence imaging, the probe displays synergistic photothermal and photodynamic therapy effects against TNBC, with minimal damage to the normal tissues. Therefore, the proposed phototheranostic probe paves a new avenue for HAase detection, and subsequently, diagnosis and imaging-guided treatment of TNBC.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer has replaced lung cancer and become the first most common cancer worldwide [1]. Triple negative breast cancer (TNBC), which accounts for approximately 15% of breast cancers, is regarded as the most aggressive subtype due to its high invasiveness, relapse and distant metastasis risks [2,3]. What's worse, TNBC lacks expression of estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), resulting in the treatment failures of endocrine or HER2 targeted therapies [4,5]. Chemotherapy is still one of the main therapeutic options for TNBC, nevertheless, chemotherapy exhibits poor response, serious systemic toxicity, and multidrug resistance [6,7]. As a result, patients with TNBC have the worst prognosis, and it is on urgent demand to develop novel diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for TNBC.

Phototheranostics, integrating fluorescence imaging (FI) and phototherapy into a single platform [8,9], have gained significant attention in cancer theranostics. Due to the inherent properties of FI including high sensitivity, easy operation and noninvasiveness, in addition to diagnosis, imaging-guided treatment can be achieved [10,11]. As for treatment, phototherapy relies on hyperthermia or reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by photosensitizers under light irradiation to achieve anticancer effect. Owing to its precise controllability, minimal invasiveness, and low systemic toxicity, phototherapy shows great potential as an anticancer strategy [12–14]. Taken together, phototheranostics will not only provide accurate cancer detection and therapeutic strategy, but also make imaging-guided phototherapy possible.

As the core of phototheranostic means, the performance of phototheranostic probes can directly affect the imaging capacity and anticancer efficacy. Firstly, probe responded to the unique tumor microenvironment is ideal for diagnosis and treatment due to its improved tumor specificity and reduced side effects. Irregular enzyme level is a typical characteristics of tumor microenvironment [15]. Hyaluronidase (HAase), a family of endoglycosidases, is highly expressed in tumor cells while less distributed in normal cells. It has been reported that HAase is correlated with tumor invasion, metastasis and progression, thus acting as a potential tumor biomarker [16,17]. Detecting intracellular HAase level may be helpful for identifying cancer and partially reflecting the malignancy grade of tumor. A variety of fluorescence probes for HAase assays have been developed [18–26], which are potential in the tumor diagnosis. However, these fluorescent probes are monofunctional and lack therapeutic properties. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is the natural substrate of HAase, which can be degraded by HAase through cleaving internal β-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine bonds. Meanwhile, HA is a ligand of CD44, an overexpressed receptor in various tumor cell membranes [27,28], endowing HA-based probes with the active tumor targeting ability. Therefore, it is advantageous to construct HA-based phototheranostic probes for evaluating HAase level, and subsequently, diagnosing cancer and guiding the treatment.

Secondly, probe with near-infrared (NIR) analytical wavelengths (650–900 nm) is especially desired in biomedical applications because of its deep tissue penetration, low background fluorescence interference, and reduced photodamage [29,30]. Indocyanine green (ICG) is an NIR imaging agent approved for human use by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Moreover, ICG can produce heat and ROS under 808 nm laser irradiation to exert photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) effects [31–33], making it a promising phototheranostic agent.

To achieving activatable FI, a fluorescence quenching unit is always introduced into the probe. During the past decades, many quenching agents have been reported [34–37]. Since biocompatibility of the probe is also a critical factor for biomedical application, polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles have arisen great interests [38–42]. The reason is that PDA is formed through self-oxidative polymerization of dopamine [39], which is a natural substance in humans. Additionally, PDA exhibits solid NIR absorption, equipped it with excellent NIR fluorescence quenching and photothermal conversion abilities [40].

With the above points in mind, herein, we developed an NIR phototheranostic probe (HA-ICG-PDA) by covalently coating ICG-decorated HA onto PDA nanoparticles, which exhibits HAase-activatable fluorescence and excellent photosensitive capacities, leading to HAase detection and NIR FI-guided PDT/PTT for TNBC (Scheme 1 ). HA-ICG-PDA itself is barely fluorescent due to the fluorescence quenching of PDA to ICG, which is favorable for achieving low background signal and high sensitivity. Upon reaction with HAase, the HA molecular chain is cleaved into fragments, along with the detach of ICG from PDA and the recovered fluorescence signal, which leads to the development of a sensitive and selective method for detecting HAase. Moreover, ICG equips the probe with the ability of converting oxygen into singlet oxygen to realize PDT. Meanwhile, owing to the solid NIR absorption contributed together by ICG and PDA, HA-ICG-PDA can act as a photothermal conversion agent for PTT. More importantly, HA-ICG-PDA is anticipated to have high biosafety because all the components contained are either endogenous substances in human body (HA and dopamine) or accredited by FDA (ICG), which can greatly facilitate the clinical translation. The features of ease preparation, high biosafety, and multiple functions make HA-ICG-PDA a promising candidate for HAase assay, and subsequently, activatable FI-guided combinational PTT/PDT towards TNBC.
Details regarding the materials and apparatus were described in the supporting information.

HA-ICG and PDA were prepared according to the previously reported methods by our team [43]. Then, HA-ICG reacted with PDA in Tris-HCl (pH 8.5) buffer solution away from light for 1 h. After that, the reaction mixture was subjected to centrifugation (13500 r/min, 15 min). HA-ICG-PDA was collected by washing the precipitation with water for three times.

The spectral responses of HA-ICG-PDA to HAase were investigated as follows. HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) was incubated with different concentrations of HAase (0–500 µg/mL) at 37 °C for 30 min. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were monitored. The fluorescence was determined with λ ex/em = 750/805 nm.

The ROS produced in the reaction system under 808 nm laser irradiation was detected with DCFH-DA. The procedure involved treating HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) with HAase (500 µg/mL) at 37 °C for 30 min. Next, activated DCFH-DA (200 nmol/L) was added, and the resulting mixture was subjected to 808 nm laser exposure (1 W/cm2) for 10 min. The fluorescence spectra of the system were collected every two minutes with λ ex = 485 nm.

Different concentrations (0–120 µg/mL) of HA-ICG-PDA were irradiated with 808 nm laser at varied power densities (0.3–2 W/cm2) for 10 min. The temperature of the solution was monitored every 30 s with an infrared thermal imager. The thermal images were collected every 2 min. The photostability of HA-ICG-PDA was explored by observing the temperature changes over five cycles of 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2), with the laser being turned on and off.

TNBC cell line (MDA-MB-231), non-TNBC cell line (MCF-7), and normal human breast epithelial cell line (MCF 10A) were treated with HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) for different periods of time (0–8 h). For studying the FI mechanism, MDA-MB-231 cells were pre-treated with 6-O-palmitoyl-L-ascorbic acid (0.5 mmol/L) or free HA (5 mg/mL). After 2 h, HA-ICG-PDA (30 µg/mL) was added and co-incubated for another 6 h. The cell imaging was performed with 640 nm excitation and 720–820 nm emission.

The adherent MDA-MB-231 cells were incubated with HA-ICG-PDA (60 µg/mL) for 6 h. After washing cells, DCFH-DA (2 µmol/L) was added and stained for 20 min. Then, cells were irradiated with 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 10 min. Cell imaging was finally conducted under a fluorescence microscope.

Cell viability was determined by standard MTT method [30]. MDA-MB-231, MCF-7 and MCF 10A cells were inoculated at 1 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well plate, the media were discarded and the cells were washed after 24 h. The cells were then incubated with HA-ICG-PDA at different concentrations (0–75 µg/mL) for 24 h. After washing, 100 µL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution was pipetted and the incubation was continued for 4–6 h. The MTT solution was then discarded and 150 µL of DMSO was added. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured on a microplate reader.

Calcein-AM/PI dyes were used to distinguish live and dead cells. To assess the killing effects of single PTT, PDT and combinational PTT/PDT, the MDA-MB-231 cells was divided into control groups and three experimental groups. The cells in PTT group were preincubated with ascorbic acid (AA) for 2 h before co-incubation with HA-ICG-PDA (60 µg/mL) for 6 h. After washing with PBS, the experimental groups were exposed to 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 10 min, with the PDT group being temperature-controlled in an ice bath during laser irradiation. Finally, cells were incubated with calcein-AM and PI for 30 min and photographed with a fluorescence microscope.

The TNBC nude mouse model was established by subcutaneously inoculating 200 µL of MDA-MB-231 cell suspension at a density of 1×107 cells/mL into the right armpits of female nude mice (5–6 weeks old). When the tumor volume reached approximately 70 mm3, HA-ICG-PDA (300 µg/mL, 100 µL) was injected into the experimental nude mice through the tail vein, and the control group was injected with the same volume of PBS. FI of the nude mice was performed at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 24 h after injection with a small animal in vivo imaging system. 24 h later, the nude mice were executed. The major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) and tumors were dissected and administered to ex vivo FI.

When the tumors grew to 70 mm3, the mice were randomized into four groups: PBS group, PBS + laser (L) group, HA-ICG-PDA group and HA-ICG-PDA + L group, respectively. The mice in each group were injected with 100 µL PBS or HA-ICG-PDA (300 µg/mL) through the tail vein. After 8 h the mice in the laser groups were irradiated with 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 10 min, during which the mice were imaged with an infrared thermos imager every two minutes and the temperature at the tumor site was recorded.

When the tumor volume was about 100 mm3 the mice were randomly separated into four groups with at least three mice in each group. The groups were PBS group, PBS + L group, HA-ICG-PDA group, and HA-ICG-PDA + L group. The mice in each group were received intravenous injection with 100 µL of PBS or HA-ICG-PDA (300 µg/mL), and the mice in the laser groups were subjected to 808 nm laser exposure (1 W/cm2) for 10 min after 8 h. The tumors were treated once every two days for a total of three times. During the 14-day treatment period, the nude mice were weighed and the tumor diameter were measured every other day, and the tumor volume was calculated according to the following formula: V = a×b 2/2, in which V is the tumor volume, a and b are the tumor length and short diameters, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis optimization and characterization of HA-ICG-PDA
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of ratios between ICG and PDA on the fluorescence response toward HAase and photothermal properties of HA-ICG-PDA were firstly investigated (Fig. S1). When the mass ratio of ICG to PDA varies from 1:4 to 4:1, the fluorescence signals of HA-ICG-PDA itself are always weak due to the fluorescence quenching of PDA to ICG. However, the addition of HAase induces fluorescence recovery of the system to different degrees. The more PDA there is, the lower the fluorescence intensity is. Meanwhile, HA-ICG-PDA with higher ratio of PDA leads to higher temperature under 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. S1B). Considering the fluorescence enhancement and photothermal effect together, mass ratio of 1:2 is used in the following study. Under such conditions, both intense fluorescence enhancement toward HAase (about 6-fold) and significant photothermal conversion capacity (ΔT > 25°C) can be achieved.

The morphology, size, and functional groups of HA-ICG-PDA were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Zeta potential, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) assays. The TEM result (insert in Fig. 1A ) reveals that HA-ICG-PDA nanoparticle is spherical, with particle size of about 180 nm. The DLS analysis (Fig. 1A) suggests that the hydration particle size of HA-ICG-PDA is 185.8 nm, with small polydispersity index of 0.023, which is in accordance with the TEM results. These observations indicate that HA-ICG-PDA has uniform particle size distribution and good dispersion. Fig. S2A shows the XRD pattern with a strong diffraction peak at 25°, indicating that HA-ICG-PDA is mainly composed of amorphous carbon. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S2B) has three characteristic peaks at 284.42, 399.16, and 530.58 eV, representing C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. In addition, four peaks at 284.42, 285.94, 286.45, and 284.40 eV appear in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S2C), which are identified as C=C/C–C, C–O, C=O, and C–N, respectively. In the N 1s spectrum, the two peaks at 399.16 and 400.57 eV are assigned to C–N–C and N–H (Fig. S2D). The peaks in O 1s profile (Fig. S2E) are classified as C=O (530.58 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.03 eV). The above results indicate that the probe HA-ICG-PDA is mainly composed of C, N and O elements, and rich in functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which is favorable for good water-solubility and high biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic responses of HA-ICG-PDA to HAase
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of PDA, HA-ICG, and HA-ICG-PDA were firstly studied. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the as-synthesized PDA has wide absorption in the range of 500–1000 nm, which is the prerequisite for excellent quenching ability and photothermal performance. The absorption spectrum of HA-ICG resembles that of ICG, demonstrating the successful decoration of HA by ICG. The probe HA-ICG-PDA shows strong absorption between 700 and 850 nm, with the maximum absorption at 810 nm, which is contributed by both PDA and ICG. The solid NIR absorption of HA-ICG-PDA is quite desired for photothermal capacity. Due to the fluorescence quenching of ICG by PDA, the probe itself is barely fluorescent, which is beneficial for the sensitive detection. Upon the addition of HAase, significant fluorescence enhancement appears at 805 nm (Fig. 1C), which resembles the fluorescence spectrum of ICG alone (Fig. S3). The reason is that HAase specifically hydrolyzes the HA molecular chain, accompanied by the detaching of ICG from PDA. As a result, the energy transfer from ICG to PDA is blocked and the fluorescence of ICG is restored. The intense fluorescence enhancement response of HA-ICG-PDA towards HAase leads to the development of a new sensitive and selective detection method for HAase.

Then, the influences of reaction time, pH and temperature on the detection system were explored. It can be seen from Fig. S4A that the fluorescence intensity of the system in the presence of HAase gradually increases with the reaction time and an approximate plateau can be reached at about 30 min. For comparison, the fluorescence of HA-ICG-PDA without HAase scarcely alters in the same period, implying that HA-ICG-PDA is stable. Besides, HA-ICG-PDA itself has weak fluorescence in the pH range of 4–9, while reaction with HAase induces similar fluorescence enhancement from pH 5 to 8 (Fig. S4B). The effect of temperature is shown in Fig. S4C, although the fluorescence of HA-ICG-PDA is barely influenced by the temperature variation from 20 to 40 °C, that of the reaction solution shows the most effective enhancement at 37 °C, which can be attributed to the maximum enzymatic activity at 37 °C. Collectively, these results demonstrate that HA-ICG-PDA performs well in physiological conditions (pH 7.4 and 37 °C), which is thus used for the determination of HAase.

Under the above-optimized conditions, the fluorescence of HA-ICG-PDA shows dependent enhancement to HAase concentration (Fig. 2A ). An ideal linear relation can be acquired when the concentration of HAase ranges from 10 to 45 µg/mL. And the linear equation is ΔF = 6.93C + 22.94, in which ΔF means the fluorescence difference with and without HAase. The detection limit was calculated to be 3.42 µg/mL by using the formula 3s/m, which is sensitive enough for detecting endogenous HAase [43–45].
By using the Lineweaver-Burk plot, the Michaelis constant (K m) of HA-ICG-PDA against HAase was calculated to be 6.69 µg/mL (Fig. S5), suggesting the strong affinity between the probe and HAase.

Then, the selective response of HA-ICG-PDA to HAase was assessed by testing the influences of latent coexisting substances, such as inorganic salts, amino acids, glutathione, AA, and some enzymes, on the fluorescence of HA-ICG-PDA. As can be seen in Fig. S6, species except for HAase have little influence on the background signal of HA-ICG-PDA, demonstrating the superior selectivity of the probe for HAase. Such high selectivity arises from the specific cleavage of HA molecular chain by HAase. Taken together, HA-ICG-PDA demonstrates excellent detection performance for HAase.

HA-ICG-PDA is equipped with photodynamic capacity due to the incorporation of ICG, which is a common NIR photosensitizer. With DCFH-DA as the ROS indicator, the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm gradually enhances with the prolonged laser irradiation period (Fig. 3A ), suggesting the formation of ROS in the reaction solution of HA-ICG-PDA with HAase. Thus, HA-ICG-PDA may have potential in the tumor PDT.

The temperature changes of HA-ICG-PDA dispersions with varied concentrations under 808 nm laser were investigated. Fig. 3B suggests that the temperature of the system is positively correlated with the concentration of HA-ICG-PDA and irradiation duration. The temperature reaches 52.9 °C at the concentration of 60 µg/mL after irradiation for 10 min, which greatly exceeds the tumor cell tolerance temperature (43 °C) [46]. For comparison, the temperature of PBS only increases to 27.5 °C, implying the significant photothermal conversion capacity of HA-ICG-PDA. Additionally, the elevated temperature depends on the power density of the laser. The higher the power density, the higher temperature can reach (Fig. 3D). The thermal images (Fig. 3C and 3E) also intuitively reveal the concentration and power-dependent temperature elevation. Photothermal stability is a critical indicator for evaluating photothermal agents. As depicted in Fig. 3F, the photothermal performance of HA-ICG-PDA exhibits neglectable changes after five cycles of repeated on-off laser irradiation, verifying the good photothermal stability of HA-ICG-PDA. These observations lie solid foundation for the application of HA-ICG-PDA in tumor PTT.

The premise of precise tumor theranostics is that probes can be availably taken in by cancer, but not normal cells. Therefore, the uptake of HA-ICG-PDA by various cell lines (MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, and MCF 10A) were studied. Upon incubation with HA-ICG-PDA, strong red fluorescence is lighted in MDA-MB-231 cells. By contrast, MCF-7 and MCF 10A emit moderate and negligible fluorescence, respectively (Fig. 4 ). Such distinct difference is attributed to the different expression levels of CD44 and HAase in these three cell lines. Specifically, TNBC cells MDA-MB-231 overexpress CD44 and HAase, thus HA-ICG-PDA can be selectively ingested by MDA-MB-231 cells through CD44-mediated endocytosis, followed by HAase-catalyzed cleavage. As a result, ICG detaches from the surface of PDA, along with the recovered fluorescence signal. While the low-level of CD44 and HAase account for the ignorable fluorescence signal in the normal cell line MCF 10A.

To further validate the mechanism of fluorescence activation in MDA-MB-231 cells, free HA and 6-O-palmitoyl-L-ascorbic acid (the inhibitor of HAase) were employed to pretreat the cells. As shown in Fig. S7, HA pretreatment partially inhibits the fluorescence signal of MDA-MB-231 cells. The occupation of CD44 binding sites by free HA blocks the uptake of HA-ICG-PDA by MDA-MB-231 cells, demonstrating that HA-ICG-PDA enters the cells through CD44-mediated endocytosis. Moreover, the inhibitor of HAase preincubation also leads to significantly decreased fluorescence signal, which suggests that the activated intracellular fluorescence indeed results from HAase induced hydrolysis of HA-ICG-PDA. The fluorescence imaging at different incubation periods (Fig. S8A and S8B) shows that the fluorescence in MDA-MB-231 cells enhances with the increased incubation duration and achieves the plateau at 6 h. Accordant results are obtained from flow cytometry assay (Fig. S8C). Together, the selective uptake and fluorescence activation of HA-ICG-PDA by TNBC, but not normal cells, are quite desired for the precise diagnosis and therapy of TNBC.

In view of laser-induced ROS production capacity of HA-ICG-PDA in tubes, the ROS level in HA-ICG-PDA-treated cells was examined by DCFH-DA staining. Compared with control group, cells with HA-ICG-PDA treatment or laser irradiation alone have neglectable fluorescence. However, bright green fluorescence is found when HA-ICG-PDA treated MDA-MB-231 cells are irradiated with 808 nm laser (Fig. S9), which proves the formation of ROS due to the photosensitive performance of HA-ICG-PDA.

Since HA-ICG-PDA displays superior photothermal effect and ROS production capacity for potential PTT and PDT, coupled with the targeted uptake by TNBC cells, the therapeutic efficacy of HA-ICG-PDA at cellular level was evaluated. As is seen in Fig. 5 , the survivals of these three cell lines are scarcely altered (> 90%) upon incubation with HA-ICG-PDA even at the concentration of 75 µg/mL for 24 h, indicating the low dark cytotoxicity of the probe. However, the laser irradiation triggers the dramatic decrease in the survival of HA-ICG-PDA treated MDA-MB-231 cells, which is ascribed to the laser-induced generation of heat and ROS by HA-ICG-PDA to achieve PTT and PDT. Moreover, the cell survival is dose-dependent, the higher concentration, the lower cell survival is obtained. Only 15% of MDA-MB-231 cells are live at the concentration of 75 µg/mL HA-ICG-PDA. Compared with MDA-MB-231 cells, MCF-7 cells keep the survival rate of 58%, while MCF 10A cells still have much higher survival (75%). In addition, the calcein-AM/PI dye was used for live/dead cell staining analysis (Fig. S10). Corresponding to the MTT data, most of MDA-MB-231 cells emit red fluorescence, representing dead cells. While green fluorescence, indicative of live cells, is found in the majority of MCF 10A cells. Such distinct survival rates among these cell lines are attributed to their different uptake of HA-ICG-PDA. The above results verify the targeted therapeutic effect of HA-ICG-PDA on TNBC cells, accompanied with greatly reduced toxicity to normal cells.

To further elucidate the respective effects of PDT and PTT on MDA-MB-231 cells, the cells were subjected to ice bath upon laser irradiation or pretreated with AA. As depicted in Fig. 5D, laser or HA-ICG-PDA treatment alone barely damages cells, again confirming the good biocompatibility of HA-ICG-PDA and the safety of the laser employed. However, partial cells are dead in the HA-ICG-PDA + laser + ice bath and HA-ICG-PDA + laser + AA groups, which results from the single PDT or PTT effect of HA-ICG-PDA, respectively. Much more dead cells are detected in HA-ICG-PDA + laser group due to the efficient therapeutic efficacy contributed by combined PDT and PTT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence and thermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo imaging can provide critical information for cancer detection and treatment because it can visualize the tumor tissues and guide the therapeutic process. Considering the excellent targeted imaging and efficient therapy capacities of HA-ICG-PDA in vitro, the fluorescence and thermal imaging in vivo was explored. As shown in Fig. 6A , gradually increasing fluorescence signal at the tumor region appears at 2 h and reaches a maximum at 8 h after intravenous injection of HA-ICG-PDA. The ex vivo fluorescence imaging of the main organs and tumor tissue (Fig. S11) reveals the much stronger fluorescence signal at tumor site compared with those at the organs. The FI results suggest that HA-ICG-PDA can target to the TNBC tissue and be activated fluorescence signal by endogenous HAase, which may hold promise for the diagnosis and imaging-guided treatment of TNBC.

Guided by the FI, laser irradiation was administered to the tumor sites at 8 h after intravenous injection of HA-ICG-PDA. As can be seen in Fig. 6B and S12, during the 10 min of irradiation, the mice with PBS injection show a small temperature fluctuation within 5 °C at the tumor site (group c), meaning laser irradiation alone has no therapeutic effect. However, the tumor region of HA-ICG-PDA injected mice (group d) exhibits significant temperature elevation (> 25 °C), meanwhile, the normal tissues show negligible temperature variation. These observations provide solid evidence for the excellent photothermal effect at the tumor region, with the negligible damage to the normal tissues.

As mentioned above, HA-ICG-PDA possesses efficient TNBC cells-suppressive efficacy in vitro, effective enrichment and photothermal capacity at tumor sites in vivo, and the in vivo antitumor efficacies of HA-ICG-PDA on TNBC-bearing mice were investigated. The mice were randomized into four groups (PBS, PBS + L, HA-ICG-PDA, and HA-ICG-PDA + L) and the treatment is proceeded as the schedule shown in Fig. 7A . The laser irradiation was administrated 8 h post-injection, which was based upon the FI result that the strongest fluorescence signal at tumor region could be reached after 8 h of HA-ICG-PDA injection. Compared to the control group (PBS), the pure laser group and HA-ICG-PDA group exhibit the same rapid growth of tumor volume (Fig. 7B and C), which indicates the neglectable side effects of laser irradiation and the phototheranostic probe. By contrast, for HA-ICG-PDA + L group, the tumor growth is obviously suppressed, which is evidenced laterally by the images and weights of the residual tumor tissues (Fig. 7D and E). These results confirm that the combination of HA-ICG-PDA and laser irradiation can exert PDT and PTT effects to inhibit tumor growth. Besides, the biosafety of the therapeutic mode was examined. Fig. 7F shows that no weight loss is noted during the treatment course. The certain blood biochemical indexes assay (Fig. S13) exhibits that the levels of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), and creatinine (CRE) have no marked fluctuation in comparison with the control group. For pathological evaluation, the H&E staining analysis of organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) dissected from each mouse after therapy was conducted. The results (Fig. S14) suggest that no distinct tissue lesions after the treatment. Taken together, HA-ICG-PDA can produce venenous ROS and hyperthermia in TNBC tissues under laser irradiation, resulting in efficient antitumor efficacy without significant damage to normal tissues.

In conclusion, a new NIR phototheranostic probe, HA-ICG-PDA, was developed for HAase detection and activatable FI-guided combinational therapy of TNBC. The probe was prepared by covalently coating ICG decorated HA onto the surface of PDA nanoparticles, which is nonfluorescent due to the quenching of PDA to ICG. Reaction with HAase leads to the liberation of ICG from PDA and thus the recovery of the fluorescence signal, which realizes the detection of HAase and FI diagnosis of HAase-overexpressed TNBC. Meanwhile, the excellent PDT, PTT and thermal imaging performances are achieved with the inherent properties of HA-ICG-PDA, including the photosensitive capacity of ICG and solid absorption in NIR region contributed together by PDA and ICG. Importantly, in vitro and in vivo study show that HA-ICG-PDA can effectively suppress tumor proliferation. Additionally, the components of the probe are either endogenous species (dopamine and HA) or agent approved for human use (ICG), which would greatly facilitate the clinical transformation. Thus, HA-ICG-PDA is expected to provide not only a detection means for HAase, but a new perspective for the next generation of tumor precise theranostics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by NSF of China (Nos. 82071969, and 21705104); the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (No. 2019L0406); the Youth Top Talent Plans of Shanxi Province (No. 20180606); Open Fund from Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University), Ministry of Education, China (No. KLMEC/SXMU-202003); and the Startup Foundation for Doctors of Shanxi Medical University (No: 03201601).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2023.100300.
